summary: This notebook implements an optimized inference pipeline for the Deep Past Challenge, focusing on Akkadian-to-English machine translation. It demonstrates how combining diverse candidate generation (beam search and multi-temperature sampling) with a weighted Minimum Bayes Risk (MBR) scorer significantly improves translation quality. The author emphasizes that extensive regex-based postprocessing is crucial to strip formatting noise, allowing the MBR algorithm to evaluate meaningful content rather than superficial inconsistencies. Readers should learn how cross-model candidate pooling, careful metric weighting, and domain-specific text normalization can boost leaderboard scores without retraining.
competition_flow: Loads the test dataset, preprocesses Akkadian transliterations, generates diverse candidates via two ByT5 models using beam search and multi-temperature sampling, applies domain-specific regex postprocessing, selects the best translation using a weighted MBR scorer, and exports the final submission CSV.
data_processing: Input normalization via regex (diacritics, gap markers, determinatives, fractions)
Extensive output postprocessing (quote normalization, commodity/fraction correction, stray mark removal, repetition/punctuation cleanup)
models: ByT5 (two custom checkpoints loaded via AutoModelForSeq2SeqLM)
ensembling: Combines candidates from two distinct ByT5 models per sample, deduplicates them, and applies a weighted Minimum Bayes Risk scorer (chrF++, BLEU, Jaccard, length bonus) to select the consensus translation.
insights: Diverse candidate generation via beam search and multi-temperature sampling provides a richer pool for MBR to find high-quality consensus outputs.
Aggressive regex postprocessing is essential to prevent MBR from penalizing superficial formatting noise instead of semantic content.
A weighted combination of chrF++, BLEU, Jaccard, and length metrics outperforms single-metric MBR for this translation task.
Bucket batching effectively stabilizes inference throughput for highly variable-length Akkadian transliterations.
critical_findings: Specific host-verified corrections are required for domain accuracy, such as mapping 5/12 shekel to ⅓ shekel 15 grains and preserving parentheses in test outputs.
Commodity regex replacements must enforce word boundaries to avoid corrupting compound terms like 'import-tax'.
Stray marks, repeated words, and inconsistent punctuation in raw model outputs severely degrade MBR scores if left uncleaned.
